answer:
**Viṣṇu Smṛti chapter 2 does not specifically prescribe anything for a king (or Kṣatriya) who has just risen from sleep.**[^1]

This chapter outlines the duties (*dharma*) and livelihoods of the four *varṇas*: Brāhmaṇas (teaching, officiating sacrifices, gifts), Kṣatriyas (constant practice with arms; protection of the earth), Vaiśyas (tending cattle, agriculture, etc.), and Śūdras (service to twice-born). It also lists common virtues like forbearance and truthfulness, and allows adopting lower *varṇa* livelihoods in distress.[^2] No daily routines, waking rituals, or similar prescriptions appear.

Chapter 3 covers *rājadharma* (kingly duties) in detail—protection of subjects, administration, taxation, justice, military strategy—but similarly lacks any reference to rising from sleep.[^3]

If this refers to a routine from another Smṛti (e.g., *Manusmṛti* 4 describes general waking ablutions, but not kings specifically), the *Viṣṇu Smṛti* chapter 2 has no such content.

[^1]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, ch. 2, [full chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/02/translation/).

[^2]: E.g., [p-f4e97b] (Kṣatriya duty); [p-e4d407] (protection); [p-00e45d] (common *dharma*).

[^3]: *Viṣṇu Smṛti*, ch. 3, [full chapter](/works/unknown-visnu-smrti-27f0d8/03/translation/).
